prompt: So our loyalty-points service is event-sourced (events land in a JSONL store as dicts with seq, type, v, data) and every consumer team has hand-rolled its own replay loop, each subtly wrong in a different way. I want ONE reusable rebuilder: module `replay.py`, class Replayer(initial) where initial is a zero-arg factory for a fresh state. r.on(type, version, fn) registers the handler for the CURRENT schema version of an event type, fn(state, data) -> new state (rebuild always uses the return value). r.upcast(type, from_version, fn) registers a data migration from from_version to from_version+1; registering the same handler slot or the same upcast step twice is a config bug and must be ValueError. r.rebuild(events, snapshot=None, strict=True) applies events in ascending seq no matter what order the list is in (the store shards, order is a lie), raises ValueError on a duplicated seq, tolerates gaps. Old producers still emit v1/v2 events, so any event below its handler's version gets chained through the upcasters step by step to reach it — a hole in the chain is ValueError, and an event NEWER than the handler is ValueError too (means the reader is out of date, fail loud). Upcasting must not scribble on the caller's event dicts. snapshot={'seq': n, 'state': ...} starts replay from that state, skipping every event with seq <= n — and the snapshot must stay pristine even if the caller mutates the rebuilt result, we cache snapshots. strict=False skips unregistered event types instead of the default KeyError. Acceptance tests: test_replay.py (they define a little points domain and pin all of the above). `python3 test_replay.py` green, test file untouched.
